tasks.title: Scrape Polyhaven and Sketchfab for downloadable prop/furniture/container 3D assets
tasks.description: We have 270 base items with 106 unique objectRoles but only ~36 actual 3D model files. Build a script (scripts/asset-scraper.ts) that: (1) Queries the Polyhaven API (api.polyhaven.com/assets?type=models) for all available model assets — filter for props, furniture, food, tools, weapons, containers, decorative items. (2) Queries the Sketchfab API (api.sketchfab.com/v3/search?type=models&downloadable=true&license=by&categories=furniture,food-drink,weapons-military,architecture) for CC-licensed downloadable models. (3) For each result, store: name, source (polyhaven/sketchfab), download URL, category, tags, license, thumbnail URL, file format (gltf/glb). (4) Output a manifest JSON file at data/available-assets-manifest.json with all results organized by category: furniture (tables, chairs, shelves, beds, desks, cabinets, wardrobes, sofas, benches, rugs, curtains, mirrors), kitchen (stoves, pots, pans, plates, cups, utensils, kettles), food (bread, fruit, vegetables, meat, cheese, pastries, cakes, beverages), tools (hammers, saws, wrenches, shovels, brooms, buckets), weapons (swords, bows, axes, shields, daggers, staffs), containers (chests, barrels, crates, baskets, bags, boxes, jars, vases), decor (paintings, candles, lanterns, clocks, books, scrolls, globes, flowers, plants, rugs, tapestries), lighting (chandeliers, sconces, floor lamps, table lamps). (5) Cross-reference against our existing 106 objectRoles and flag which roles already have assets vs which need new ones. (6) Prioritize: quest-critical items first, then merchant inventory items, then furniture, then decorative.

tasks.title: Download and integrate top-priority 3D assets into the project
tasks.description: Using the manifest from task 1, download and integrate assets. (1) Download the top 100-150 most useful assets (prioritizing furniture, food, tools, containers, and decor) from Polyhaven and Sketchfab. Save to client/public/assets/models/props/polyhaven/ and client/public/assets/models/props/sketchfab/ respectively. (2) For each downloaded asset, ensure it has a .gltf or .glb file plus textures in a subdirectory. (3) Optimize large models: cap triangle count at 10K, compress textures to max 1024x1024. (4) Register each new asset in the visual_assets collection via a new migration (server/migrations/024-register-new-prop-assets.ts). (5) Update data/base-asset-collections.json to map objectRoles to the new assets — replace placeholder procedural references with real model references. (6) For furniture specifically, download at least: 3 table variants, 3 chair variants, 2 bed variants, 2 shelf variants, 2 cabinet/wardrobe variants, 1 desk, 1 sofa/bench, 1 rug, 2 lighting fixtures (chandelier, sconce/candle). These are critical for interior enhancement.

tasks.title: Expand the base item catalog with new categories and items
tasks.description: Expand from 270 to 500+ base items covering new categories needed for enriched gameplay. Create server/migrations/025-expand-base-items.ts that seeds new items: (1) FURNITURE (new category, ~40 items): wooden_table, round_table, dining_table, desk, wooden_chair, armchair, stool, bench, single_bed, double_bed, bunk_bed, wardrobe, cabinet, bookshelf, shelf_unit, dresser, nightstand, sofa, loveseat, rug, curtain, mirror, painting, clock, chandelier, wall_sconce, floor_lamp, coat_rack, umbrella_stand, piano, grandfather_clock. Each with appropriate objectRole, weight, value, material. (2) KITCHEN/COOKING (~25 items): stove, oven, cooking_pot, frying_pan, kettle, plate, bowl, cup, mug, glass, fork, knife_utensil, spoon, cutting_board, rolling_pin, mixing_bowl, pitcher, teapot, mortar_pestle, spice_rack. (3) FOOD expansion (~30 items beyond existing): croissant, baguette, cheese_wheel, apple, pear, grapes, fish_fillet, chicken_leg, stew, soup, salad, cake, pie, tart, chocolate, honey_jar, olive_oil, wine_bottle, beer_mug, coffee_cup, tea_cup, milk_bottle, jam_jar, butter, egg_basket, sausage. (4) STATIONERY/OFFICE (~15 items): book, open_book, scroll, quill_pen, ink_bottle, parchment, letter, envelope, wax_seal, ledger, map_scroll, globe, hourglass, abacus, stamp. (5) GARDEN/NATURE (~15 items): flower_pot, watering_can, garden_hoe, rake, wheelbarrow, hay_bale, flower_bouquet, herb_bundle, mushroom, seed_bag, beehive, bird_cage, fishing_rod, net, basket. (6) DECORATIVE (~20 items): tapestry, banner, flag, trophy, statue_small, bust, vase, ceramic_pot, woven_basket, dreamcatcher, wind_chime, music_box, compass, telescope, binoculars, magnifying_glass, pocket_watch, monocle, top_hat, walking_cane. (7) Set isBase=true, appropriate worldType (null for universal), and map each to an objectRole. All food/drink items should have languageLearningData with French translations.

tasks.title: Expand business type definitions with specialized shop types and service businesses
tasks.description: The schema defines 32 business types but most aren't fully realized with interiors, work behaviors, or merchant inventories. Expand and specialize: (1) Add new business types to the BusinessType union in shared/schema.ts: 'Cafe', 'Bookstore', 'HardwareStore', 'ArtSupplyStore', 'ClothingStore', 'FurnitureStore', 'FloristShop', 'MusicShop', 'Butcher', 'FishMarket', 'WineShop', 'CandleShop', 'PotteryShop', 'Cobbler', 'Barber', 'Tailor', 'PrintShop', 'PostOffice'. (2) For each new type, define in BuildingInteriorGenerator.ts: room dimensions, furniture layout (what furniture pieces and where), color scheme. (3) For each new type, add to merchant-inventory.ts: what items they sell (Bookstore sells books/scrolls/stationery, HardwareStore sells tools, ArtSupplyStore sells craft materials, Florist sells flowers/plants, etc.). (4) Add work behavior sequences in BusinessBehaviorSystem.ts for each type (Cafe: brew_coffee → serve_customer → wipe_counter; Bookstore: shelve_books → assist_customer → read; etc.). (5) Update BusinessDialog.tsx dropdown to include all new types. (6) Each business type should have vocabulary relevance for language learning — e.g., visiting a Boulangerie teaches bread/pastry vocab, visiting a Librairie teaches book/reading vocab.

tasks.title: Add public building type with schools, city halls, libraries, and post offices
tasks.description: Currently buildingType is only 'residence' | 'business' | 'vacant'. Add 'public' as a fourth type. (1) Update the lots table buildingType enum in shared/schema.ts to include 'public'. (2) Define a PublicBuildingType union: 'School', 'CityHall', 'Library', 'PostOffice', 'Museum', 'Theater', 'Courthouse', 'FireStation', 'PoliceStation', 'TrainStation', 'Church'. (3) Add a public_buildings collection to the schema with fields: id, worldId, settlementId, name, publicBuildingType, lotId, capacity, operatingHours, staffIds (array), description. (4) Add CRUD endpoints for public buildings in server/routes.ts. (5) In BuildingInteriorGenerator.ts, add interior layouts for each type: School (desks in rows, teacher desk, blackboard, bookshelves), CityHall (reception desk, meeting room with long table, office furniture, flag/banner), Library (many bookshelves in rows, reading tables, study desks, card catalog), PostOffice (service counter, mail slots, parcel shelves), Museum (display cases, pedestals, paintings on walls). (6) In ProceduralBuildingGenerator.ts, add exterior specs for each type (School: 2 floors, wide; Library: 3 floors, stone; CityHall: 3 floors, grand entrance). (7) Generate 2-4 public buildings per settlement during world generation. (8) Staff public buildings with NPCs who work there (teachers at School, librarians at Library, clerks at CityHall).

tasks.title: Replace placeholder box furniture in interiors with actual 3D models
tasks.description: BuildingInteriorGenerator.ts currently creates all furniture as colored MeshBuilder boxes. Replace with real 3D models. (1) Create a FurnitureModelLoader utility that maps furniture type strings ('table', 'chair', 'bed', 'shelf', 'counter', 'stool', 'barrel', 'crate', 'chest', 'wardrobe', 'workbench', 'anvil', 'bookshelf', 'altar', 'pew', 'desk', 'cabinet', 'rug', 'chandelier') to asset IDs in the world's AssetCollection config3D. (2) In BuildingInteriorGenerator.ts, replace each MeshBuilder.CreateBox call for furniture with a model clone from the FurnitureModelLoader. (3) If no model is available for a furniture type, fall back to the existing colored box (don't break anything). (4) Scale models appropriately — furniture should be roughly human-scale (tables ~0.8m high, chairs ~0.5m seat height, beds 2m long). (5) Apply correct rotation so furniture faces the right direction (chairs face tables, beds align with walls, shelves against walls). (6) Add collision meshes for all furniture models so the player can't walk through them. (7) Keep the existing material color scheme as a tint/filter on the loaded models to maintain per-building-type aesthetic variation.

tasks.title: Enlarge building interiors and add multi-room layouts for larger buildings
tasks.description: Current interiors are single rooms that feel cramped. Enhance: (1) Increase base room dimensions by 30-50%: small residences 9x9m (was 6x6), medium 12x12m (was 8x8), large 16x16m (was 12x12), shops 14x14m (was 10x10), taverns 18x16m (was 14x12), temples 20x24m (was 16x20). (2) For buildings with 2+ floors in their exterior spec, generate a multi-room layout: ground floor (main room + back room separated by a wall with doorway) and optionally an upstairs area (accessible via stairs mesh). (3) For residences: ground floor = living room + kitchen area; upstairs = bedroom(s). (4) For businesses: ground floor = customer-facing shop area + back room (storage/office); some have upstairs living quarters. (5) Add wall partitions as thin box meshes with doorway cutouts. (6) Add a staircase mesh (simple ramp or stepped geometry) connecting floors. (7) Populate each room section with furniture appropriate to its function (kitchen: stove, table, chairs, shelves with dishes; bedroom: bed, wardrobe, nightstand; shop floor: display counter, shelves with merchandise; storage: crates, barrels). (8) Update InteriorNPCManager to position NPCs in the correct room based on their role (shopkeeper at counter, cook in kitchen, resident in living room during day / bedroom at night).

tasks.title: Implement visual day/night cycle with lighting and sky color transitions
tasks.description: GameTimeManager.ts tracks time but there are no visual changes. Implement: (1) Create client/src/components/3DGame/DayNightCycleManager.ts that subscribes to GameTimeManager's time_of_day_changed events. (2) Sun position: Move the directional light in a circular arc — east at dawn (6am), overhead at noon, west at dusk (6pm), below horizon at night. Use a HemisphericLight for ambient plus a DirectionalLight for sun. (3) Sky color gradient: dawn = warm orange/pink (#FF9966 → #FFccaa), morning/afternoon = clear blue (#87CEEB), evening = deep orange/purple (#FF6633 → #993399), night = dark blue/black (#001133 → #000011). Interpolate smoothly between periods using BABYLON.Color3.Lerp with the fractional hour. (4) Ambient light intensity: dawn 0.3, day 1.0, dusk 0.4, night 0.15. (5) Shadow direction: rotate shadow generator's light source to match sun position. (6) Fog: light blue fog during day (visibility 500m), thicker gray fog at dawn/dusk (300m), minimal at night. (7) Interior lighting: when player is inside a building, override with warm interior lighting (orange-tinted point lights) regardless of outdoor time. (8) Add a time-of-day indicator HUD element: small clock icon with current time (e.g., '14:30') and a sun/moon icon. (9) Street lamps and building windows should glow at night (emissive materials activated when hour >= 19 or hour < 6).

tasks.title: Implement time-of-day HUD indicator and time controls
tasks.description: Add a visible time display to the game HUD. (1) In BabylonGUIManager.ts, add a small panel in the top-right (near minimap) showing: current time in 24h format (e.g., '14:30'), a sun/moon icon that transitions based on time period, the current day number ('Day 3'). (2) Add a time speed control: clicking the clock cycles through 1x, 2x, 4x, 8x speed (calls GameTimeManager.setTimeScale()). (3) Show a subtle gradient bar under the clock representing the full day cycle with a marker for current time position. (4) At dawn and dusk, briefly flash a notification: 'Dawn is breaking...' / 'Night is falling...' to orient the player. (5) Business operating hours should be visible: when looking at a business, show 'Open' or 'Closed' based on game time (most businesses open 8am-8pm). (6) NPCs should reference time naturally in conversation: 'Good morning!' before noon, 'Good evening!' after 6pm, in the target language.

tasks.title: Enforce NPC town boundary confinement and fix wilderness wandering
tasks.description: NPCs sometimes wander into the wilderness and never return. Fix: (1) In NPCScheduleSystem.ts, add a settlement boundary check. Calculate the bounding box of all sidewalk nodes for each settlement, then add a 20-unit padding. If an NPC's target position is outside this boundary, reject the target and pick a new one within bounds. (2) Add a periodic boundary enforcement check in the NPC update loop (every 5 seconds): if an NPC's current position is more than 30 units outside the settlement boundary, teleport them to the nearest sidewalk node. (3) When an NPC has no valid goal (no sidewalk nodes, no buildings), default to idling at the settlement center rather than walking in a random direction. (4) Add a maximum wander distance parameter (default 50 units from settlement center) that caps how far the 'wander_sidewalk' goal can take an NPC. (5) When NPCs finish a goal at a building, their next goal should prefer nearby buildings/sidewalks rather than distant ones. (6) Log a warning when an NPC is forced back into bounds so we can debug generation issues. (7) For NPCs that are 'going home' at night, ensure the pathfinding doesn't route them through wilderness — if no sidewalk path exists, teleport them directly.

tasks.title: Properly assign NPC occupations during world generation for all characters
tasks.description: Characters currently start with null occupations and only get them when hired by businesses. Fix: (1) In genealogy-generator.ts or world-generator.ts, after all characters are created but before business generation, assign default occupations based on age and settlement context: children (0-15) = 'Student' or 'Child', elderly (65+) = 'Retired', young adults without jobs (16-24) = 'Apprentice' or 'Student', adults without businesses (25-64) = contextual defaults like 'Farmer', 'Laborer', 'Homemaker', 'Artisan', 'Fisherman' based on settlement type. (2) During business creation, when an owner/employee is assigned, update their occupation to the specific role (e.g., 'Baker', 'Shopkeeper', 'Pharmacist', 'Innkeeper'). (3) Add an occupation field to the character generation template that's never null — always at least 'Unemployed' for working-age adults. (4) Add occupation variety: for each settlement, distribute occupations realistically — not everyone works in a business. Some are farmers, some are craftspeople working from home, some are retired. (5) Ensure the hiring-system.ts respects existing occupations — don't hire someone who already has a specialized role unless they're upgrading. (6) Store occupation history on the character's businessHistory array.

tasks.title: Enhance character detail view to show workplace, residence, and business ownership
tasks.description: CharacterDetailView.tsx currently doesn't show where a character works, lives, or what they own. Fix: (1) Add a 'Residence' section: query the residences collection for any residence where residentIds includes this character's ID. Display the address and a clickable link to the residence. (2) Add a 'Workplace' section: query the businesses collection for any business where this character is referenced as an employee (check businessData.employees array or vacancies). Display the business name, type, and role. Also check if the character's occupation field references a business. (3) Add a 'Businesses Owned' section: query businesses where ownerId matches this character's ID. Display each business name, type, founded year, and status. (4) Add an 'Age' display that calculates current age from birthYear and the world's current year. (5) Show the character's daily schedule summary if available: 'Works at [Business] from 9am-5pm, Lives at [Address]'. (6) Make business and residence names clickable to navigate to their respective detail views.

tasks.title: Enhance business detail view to show owner, employees, and inventory
tasks.description: BusinessDetailCard.tsx doesn't show owner or employees. Fix: (1) Add an 'Owner' section: resolve ownerId to a character name and display with a clickable link to CharacterDetailView. Show the owner's portrait thumbnail if available. (2) Add an 'Employees' section: query characters whose occupation references this business, or whose businessHistory includes this businessId with active=true. Display each employee's name, role, and a clickable link. (3) Add an 'Inventory' section: if this business has merchant inventory, show a summary (number of items, total value, item categories). (4) Add 'Operating Hours' display (default 8am-8pm, or custom if set). (5) Add a 'Location' section showing the lot address and settlement. (6) Add a 'Revenue' or 'Status' indicator if economic data is available. (7) Ensure the BusinessDialog.tsx creation dropdown includes ALL business types from the schema (currently only 18 of 32+ types shown).

tasks.title: Create residence detail view showing residents and household information
tasks.description: No residence detail view exists — only a creation dialog. Create one: (1) Create client/src/components/ResidenceDetailCard.tsx (or enhance the existing dialog). (2) Show basic info: address, residence type, number of rooms. (3) Add 'Residents' section: resolve residentIds to character names and display each with clickable links. Show each resident's relationship to others in the household (spouse, child, parent). (4) Add 'Owner' section: resolve ownerIds to character names with clickable links. (5) Add 'Household Items' section: if the interior item system has items assigned to this residence, show a summary. (6) Show a visual: the residence's exterior image if captured, or the building type icon. (7) Add 'Location' section showing lot address and settlement. (8) Wire the residence detail view into the editor's navigation — clicking a residence from the map or from a character's profile should open this view.

tasks.title: Fill businesses with correct owner and employee NPCs during operating hours
tasks.description: Businesses need to be staffed with the right NPCs. (1) In InteriorNPCManager.ts, when the player enters a business, look up the business record to find ownerId and employee character IDs. Spawn those specific NPCs inside (not random ones). (2) Position the owner behind the counter/register. Position employees at their work stations based on business type (cook at stove in Restaurant, assistant at shelves in Shop, waiter at tables in Cafe). (3) Add time-of-day awareness: businesses are staffed from 8am to 8pm (game time). If the player enters outside business hours, the business should appear empty or have a 'Closed' sign and locked door. (4) For each business type, define a minimum staff requirement: Shop/Bakery = 1 (owner), Restaurant/Cafe = 2 (owner + cook or waiter), Hotel/Inn = 2 (owner + maid), Bar = 2 (owner + barkeep). (5) If a business doesn't have enough assigned employees, generate a generic 'assistant' NPC to fill the gap. (6) Staff NPCs should have business-appropriate greetings and behavior: 'Welcome to [Business Name]! What can I get you?' in the target language. (7) When the player talks to a business NPC, the conversation context should include the business type and available services/merchandise.

tasks.title: Fill residences with residents during evening and night hours
tasks.description: Residences should have their residents at home in the evenings. (1) In InteriorNPCManager.ts, when the player enters a residence, check if it's evening/night (after 6pm game time). If so, spawn the resident NPCs inside. (2) Position residents doing contextual activities based on time: 6pm-8pm = living room (eating, chatting), 8pm-10pm = living room (reading, relaxing), 10pm-6am = bedroom (sleeping — NPC in bed with 'sleep' animation or idle pose). (3) During work hours (9am-5pm), residences should be mostly empty (residents are at work/school). Homemakers, retired, or young children may still be home. (4) Add activity props: if a resident is 'eating', place food items on the table; if 'reading', place a book in their hands/on the table. (5) Children should be at school during school hours (8am-3pm) and at home otherwise. (6) Allow the player to knock on doors before entering residences — if it's late at night, the resident might not answer or might be annoyed (this adds realism and language practice). (7) Residents should react naturally to the player entering: 'Oh, hello! Come in!' during day, 'It's late, what do you need?' at night.

tasks.title: Implement player photography system with photo book and noun labeling
tasks.description: Add a photography feature for vocabulary learning. (1) Create client/src/components/3DGame/PhotoCameraSystem.ts. When the player presses C (camera key), enter photo mode: hide HUD, show a camera viewfinder overlay (centered rectangle with subtle vignette border), allow the player to look around freely. (2) Press Enter/Click to take a photo: use BABYLON.Tools.CreateScreenshotUsingRenderTargetAsync() to capture the current camera view at 1280x720. (3) Identify the subject: cast a ray from screen center to determine what the player is photographing. Check mesh metadata for: item (objectRole → base item name → French translation), NPC (character name), building (business/residence name), nature feature (tree, rock, river, etc.). (4) Label the photo with the identified noun in both French and English: 'un arbre / a tree', 'la boulangerie / the bakery', 'Monsieur Dupont'. (5) Store photos in a PhotoBook: array of { imageDataUrl, subject, frenchLabel, englishLabel, timestamp, location }. Persist to playthrough data via API. (6) Create client/src/components/3DGame/BabylonPhotoBookPanel.ts: a GUI panel showing a grid of photo thumbnails. Clicking a photo shows it full-size with the label. (7) Photos contribute to vocabulary learning: each unique noun photographed is added to the player's vocabulary journal. (8) Add a photo counter HUD element showing 'Photos: 12/50' or similar.

tasks.title: Add photography quest objectives and integrate with quest system
tasks.description: Wire the photography system into quests. (1) Add a new canonical objective type 'photograph_subject' to shared/quest-objective-types.ts with fields: targetSubject (string), targetCategory ('item'|'npc'|'building'|'nature'), requiredCount, currentCount. (2) Add seed quest templates in quest-seed-generator.ts: 'Photo Safari' (photograph 5 different items), 'Portrait Gallery' (photograph 3 different NPCs), 'Architectural Tour' (photograph 3 different buildings), 'Nature Photographer' (photograph 3 natural features — trees, rocks, water). (3) When a photo is taken, fire a 'photo_taken' event on GameEventBus with the subject and category. QuestCompletionEngine listens and increments matching photograph_subject objectives. (4) For vocabulary-focused photo quests: 'Visual Dictionary' — photograph 10 unique nouns to build your vocabulary. 'Vocabulary Scavenger Hunt' — photograph specific items listed in the quest (e.g., 'photograph: un pain, une fleur, un livre'). (5) Photo quests work well because they combine exploration (finding subjects) with vocabulary (learning the French word). (6) Add minimap markers for photo quest targets when the target is a specific location or building.

tasks.title: Implement a player vehicle (bicycle or horse) for faster travel
tasks.description: Players need faster travel between distant locations. (1) Create client/src/components/3DGame/PlayerVehicle.ts. Implement a bicycle as the default vehicle (fits multiple world types; simpler than a horse). (2) The bicycle is obtained from a specific location (e.g., a shed near the player's spawn, or purchased from a shop for 50g). Once obtained, the player can press V to mount/dismount. (3) While mounted: movement speed increases 3x (from ~5 m/s walk to ~15 m/s), camera position raises slightly, a bicycle mesh is shown beneath the player (simple model or placeholder cylinder). (4) While mounted: cannot enter buildings (auto-dismount at doors), cannot talk to NPCs (must dismount first), cannot pick up items. (5) Add a simple bicycle 3D model to client/public/assets/models/props/ (or use a placeholder). (6) The bicycle stays where the player dismounts it — show the mesh at the dismount position. Player must return to it to remount. (7) For medieval-fantasy worlds, substitute a horse instead of a bicycle (same mechanics, different model and sound). (8) Add a mount/dismount animation transition (brief 0.5s freeze + camera shift). (9) Ensure mounted movement still follows terrain height and collision. (10) The vehicle could be a quest reward from an early quest: 'The Settlement Courier' — deliver a message, receive a bicycle as reward.

tasks.title: Add quest objective fallback system for uncompletable objectives
tasks.description: When a quest objective can't be completed (missing location, item, NPC, or mechanism), automatically provide a fallback. (1) Create client/src/components/3DGame/QuestFallbackManager.ts. On quest activation, validate each objective: does the target location exist? Does the target NPC exist? Does the required item exist in the world? Is the game mechanic implemented? (2) Define fallback rules per objective type: visit_location with no position → fallback to 'explore any location'; collect_item with no spawned items → fallback to 'buy the item from a merchant' or 'talk to an NPC about the item'; craft_item with no crafting station → fallback to 'collect materials and bring to a craftsperson NPC'; defeat_enemies with no enemies → fallback to 'talk to the guard about threats'; escort_npc with broken pathfinding → fallback to 'meet NPC at destination'. (3) When a fallback is triggered, mark the objective with a fallback flag: { isFallback: true, originalType, fallbackType, fallbackReason }. (4) In the quest UI (QuestOfferPanel, BabylonQuestTracker), show a '?' icon or yellow highlight next to fallback objectives so the player knows it was adjusted. (5) Show a brief toast: 'Quest objective adjusted: [original] → [fallback]'. (6) Log all fallbacks for debugging so we can fix the root cause. (7) Fallback objectives should always be conversation-based since those are the most reliably completable.

tasks.title: Expand XP and money rewards for all quest completions
tasks.description: Ensure all quests reward both XP and gold, and that the economy is balanced. (1) Audit all 52 quests in world 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f: every quest should have both experienceReward > 0 and rewards.gold > 0. For quests missing gold rewards, add them scaled to difficulty: beginner = 10-20g, intermediate = 25-50g, advanced = 60-100g. (2) Update quest-seed-generator.ts to always generate gold rewards alongside XP. (3) Wire quest completion to actually award gold: in BabylonGame.ts where quest_completed events are handled, add gold to playerGold and persist. (4) Add gold reward display to the quest completion notification: 'Quest Complete! +25 XP, +15 gold'. (5) When the player runs out of gold, they should still be able to complete quests — gold is helpful but not required. (6) Add a 'low funds' warning when gold drops below 10. (7) Make sure buying from merchants correctly deducts gold, and selling correctly adds gold (verify BabylonShopPanel integration). (8) Player starts with 100g; a full playthrough of 50 quests should yield roughly 1500-2500g total, while spending on items/services consumes maybe 500-1000g.

tasks.title: Wire XP gains to skill tree progression and level-up rewards
tasks.description: XP is tracked but doesn't meaningfully affect the skill tree. Fix: (1) In LanguageGamificationTracker.ts, ensure every XP gain event checks for level-ups against LEVEL_THRESHOLDS. (2) On level-up, fire a 'level_up' event on GameEventBus with the new level. (3) In BabylonSkillTreePanel.ts, when a level-up occurs, check which skill tree nodes are now unlockable (based on the node's level/condition requirements). Auto-unlock nodes whose conditions are met. (4) Add a level-up celebration: golden particle burst, fanfare sound, large toast notification showing 'Level Up! Level X — [Tier Name]'. (5) Each level-up should grant tangible benefits: +10 max inventory slots (every 3 levels), +5% merchant discount (every 5 levels), new conversation topics unlocked, access to harder quests. (6) Show current level and XP progress bar in the HUD (near the gold display). Format: 'Lv. 5 ████░░ 340/500 XP'. (7) Skill tree nodes should show their unlock condition clearly: 'Requires: 50 words learned' or 'Requires: Level 8'. (8) Ensure all XP sources are accounted for: quest completion, conversation turns, vocabulary learned, pronunciation practice, photos taken, signs read, items examined.

tasks.title: Add spending sinks and economic pressure so gold matters
tasks.description: Gold currently accumulates with few uses. Add meaningful spending: (1) Inn/Hotel stays: resting at an Inn costs 5-15g but fully restores any status effects and advances time to morning (useful if it's late and businesses are closed). (2) Fast travel: if a transit station or carriage service exists, pay 10g to instantly travel between settlements. (3) Hint purchases: if the player is stuck on a quest, they can ask an NPC for a hint for 5g ('Where can I find [item]?' → NPC gives directions). (4) Vocabulary tutoring: pay a teacher NPC 20g for a tutoring session that teaches 5 specific vocabulary words relevant to current quests. (5) Item repair: if durability exists, repair worn items at a blacksmith for 10-30g. (6) Gifts: buying gifts for NPCs (10-50g) improves relationship, which unlocks better conversation topics and quest rewards. (7) Premium items: some merchants sell rare/useful items at high prices (100-300g) that are worth saving for. (8) Add a 'broke' state: when gold hits 0, show a persistent indicator. The player can still complete conversation quests but can't buy items or services. They must earn gold through quest rewards or selling found items.

tasks.title: Add ambient life behaviors: NPCs doing chores, eating, socializing contextually
tasks.description: NPCs should feel alive with contextual behaviors beyond walking between buildings. (1) In NPCScheduleSystem.ts, expand the goal types beyond go_to_building/wander_sidewalk/idle/visit_friend. Add: eat_at_restaurant (go to restaurant, sit at table, idle for 5-10 min), shop_at_store (go to shop, browse near shelves for 3-5 min), pray_at_church (go to church, kneel at pew), read_at_library (go to library, sit at desk), tend_garden (if has garden, kneel and work), sweep_street (if has broom role, sweep animation near building), carry_goods (walk between buildings with a purpose), chat_with_neighbor (two NPCs stop and face each other for 2-3 min). (2) Add visible props for activities: NPC eating = plate on table in front of them, NPC reading = book in hand, NPC working = tool in hand. (3) NPCs in residences during evening should cycle through: cook dinner (6-7pm, in kitchen area), eat (7-8pm, at table), relax (8-10pm, in living area), sleep (10pm+, in bedroom). (4) Add NPC idle animations beyond standing: sitting (on chairs/benches), leaning (against walls), looking around, gesturing (during conversations). (5) Weekend/rest day behaviors: NPCs spend more time socializing, shopping, and at public buildings instead of working.

tasks.title: Add weather system with rain, clouds, and atmospheric effects
tasks.description: Weather adds atmosphere and vocabulary opportunities. (1) Create client/src/components/3DGame/WeatherSystem.ts. Define weather states: clear, partly_cloudy, overcast, light_rain, heavy_rain, fog, snow (if climate appropriate). (2) Weather changes every 2-4 game hours randomly, with smooth transitions. (3) Visual effects: rain = particle system of falling droplets + wet ground material (darker, slightly reflective), clouds = moving cloud plane above the terrain with varying opacity, fog = Babylon.js scene fog density increase, snow = white particle system + ground color shift to white. (4) Lighting interaction: overcast reduces ambient light to 0.6x, rain further to 0.4x. (5) NPC behavior: during heavy rain, NPCs seek shelter (go to nearest building, stand under awnings). During clear weather, more NPCs are outside. (6) Sound effects: rain sounds when raining, wind when overcast. (7) Weather vocabulary: integrate weather into NPC conversations — 'Il pleut aujourd\'hui' (It's raining today). Add weather-related vocabulary items (umbrella, raincoat). (8) Add a weather indicator to the HUD next to the time display (sun icon, cloud icon, rain icon). (9) Some quest objectives could be weather-dependent: 'Take a photo of rain' for photography quests.

tasks.title: Add animal NPCs (cats, dogs, birds) for world ambiance and vocabulary
tasks.description: Animals make the world feel alive and provide vocabulary targets. (1) Create client/src/components/3DGame/AmbientAnimalSystem.ts. Spawn simple animal meshes around settlements: dogs (wander near residences, follow owners), cats (sit on windowsills, wander rooftops and alleys), birds (perch on roofs, fly between trees), chickens (near farms), fish (in water features). (2) Use simple low-poly models or even billboard sprites for animals. (3) Animals have basic AI: dogs wander within 20m of a home, cats patrol along walls and roofs, birds occasionally fly from one perch to another. (4) Animals are interactable: player can approach and examine them, learning the French vocabulary word (un chien, un chat, un oiseau, un poisson, une poule). (5) Some animals could be pets of specific NPCs — NPC conversations might reference their pet. (6) Animals contribute to photography quests (photographing 'un chat' counts as a vocabulary photo). (7) Spawn 5-10 animals per settlement based on settlement size and building types (farms have more chickens, residences have cats/dogs). (8) Animals don't have conversations but can 'respond' with sounds (bark, meow, chirp) when the player interacts.

tasks.title: Add outdoor furniture and market stalls to settlement exteriors
tasks.description: Settlement exteriors need more detail beyond buildings. (1) In the procedural world generation, add outdoor props near buildings: benches along streets, tables and chairs outside cafes/restaurants (outdoor seating), market stalls in the town square (simple canopy structures with item displays), flower boxes under windows, street lamps (already partially exist), well or fountain at settlement center, notice boards at intersections, trash bins, post boxes, planters with flowers. (2) Create a SettlementDetailGenerator.ts that places these props along sidewalks and at building frontages based on the building type (restaurants get outdoor tables, shops get display stands, residences get flower boxes and benches). (3) Outdoor furniture should use real 3D models where available. (4) Market stalls should display items from nearby shop merchants — clicking a stall item lets the player examine or buy it. (5) Add settlement-center gathering areas: a town square with a fountain, benches arranged in a circle, and a notice board. NPCs should gather here during idle time. (6) These props create more photography targets and vocabulary opportunities.

tasks.title: Add ambient sound system with environmental audio tied to location and time
tasks.description: Sound design dramatically improves immersion. (1) Create client/src/components/3DGame/AmbientSoundManager.ts. Define sound zones: town (market chatter, footsteps, distant conversations), nature (birds, wind, rustling leaves, water), interior (muffled outside sounds, creaking floor, fire crackling), night (crickets, owls, fewer voices). (2) Use Babylon.js Sound objects with spatial audio where appropriate — sounds near buildings, water near rivers/fountains. (3) Time-of-day audio layers: morning birdsong (6-10am), midday bustle (10am-2pm), evening calm (6-8pm), night silence with crickets (10pm-5am). (4) Business-specific ambient sounds: Bakery = sizzling/clanking, Blacksmith = hammering, Restaurant = plates/chatter, Church = bells (on the hour). (5) Player action sounds: footstep sounds on different surfaces (grass, stone, wood), door open/close, item pickup, photo shutter click, inventory open/close. (6) Volume attenuation: sounds fade with distance, interior sounds are muffled when outside and vice versa. (7) Use free sound assets from Freesound.org (FREESOUND_CLIENT_ID is already in .env). (8) Add a volume slider in settings.

tasks.title: End-to-end validation: verify all new systems work together in world 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f
tasks.description: After all tasks complete, do a comprehensive integration validation. (1) Load world 69bba18ce2e6d5c1cb3c7a9f and verify: time-of-day cycle is visually working (sunrise → noon → sunset → night with proper lighting), weather changes occur, ambient sounds play. (2) Check businesses: enter 3+ different business types, verify the correct owner/employees are inside, verify merchants have inventory, buy and sell items, verify gold changes. (3) Check residences: enter 2+ residences in the evening, verify residents are home doing activities. (4) Check NPCs: observe 5+ NPCs for 2 in-game hours — verify they stay within town bounds, go to work in the morning, visit shops, return home at night, don't wander into wilderness. (5) Check photography: take 5+ photos of different subjects (item, NPC, building, animal, nature), verify they appear in the photo book with French labels. (6) Check vehicles: obtain bicycle/horse, verify speed increase, verify auto-dismount at buildings. (7) Check quest objectives: verify all quest types show minimap markers, verify fallback system activates for any broken objectives. (8) Check editor: verify character detail shows workplace/residence, business detail shows owner/employees, residence detail shows residents. (9) Check economy: verify starting gold, quest gold rewards, XP level-ups, skill tree unlocks. (10) Check interiors: verify furniture is 3D models not boxes, verify multi-room layouts for larger buildings. Write results to docs/ENHANCEMENT_INTEGRATION_RESULTS.md.
